comment.body: More of a macro thought for the longer term, but isn’t the ending of QT actually bearish. Speaks to a lack of liquidity with regards to bank reserves, which will be exacerbated by the refilling of the TGA. Also, if I understand this correctly, this was mitigated the last few years due to the large amount of $ in RRP, but that’s been drawn down, so there is not much left to back stop the liquidity.

comment.body:
Also, we all signed up for a course to increase the effectiveness of our trading by being more profitable. Her analysis is the finished product of where we hope to be after learning this course. However, if there is an instance where that analysis goes awry, then it’s a good learning experience to understand what went wrong or what was misunderstood. You could have all the best analysis there is, but if you don’t know how to implement it effectively into a trading strategy, then it’s good for nothing. That is just as important as the analysis itself.

So really, the race track is the market, the race car is her analysis, and how to drive the race car is how to apply this analysis into a successful trading strategy, imho.

You could have all the best analysis there is, but if you don’t know how to implement it effectively into a trading strategy, then it’s good for nothing. That is just as important as the analysis itself.

So really, the race track is the market, the race car is her analysis, and how to drive the race car is how to apply this analysis into a successful trading strategy, imho.

comment.body:
The problem with your and othercomments in general is that you put a one-dimensional framing on a multidimensional problem. Short volatility for example can have a multitude of expressions (single stock volatility, index volatility, VIX), through a multitude of instruments or option structures/baskets. Any options trader worth their salt shorts overpriced volatility, not just volatility. So there is a lot more thought and analysis behind a mere "short volatility". So unless you don't know what she was shorting and you don't explain what you are watching to come to your conclusion your questions will remain unanswered. And like stated before, things change. If a big position is unwound or bought, these flows will change the profile and might trump the premarket analysis.
And maybe a more general note: We all signed up for the course in racing a Formula 1 car and yet people keep asking if they should go left or right at the next curve. Or keep asking what this steering wheel is everybody talks about. Everybody here wants up or down, bull or bear, after this OpEX this, after that OpEx that. Fact is nobody knows and even the insiders can be run over. So her analysis is like a torch in a dark basement wherethe furniture is constantly rearranged. If you don't have your own metrics and sensors to gauge you will lack conviction in what you do and then you will sell out of positions or buy positions because you interpreted something Alma said in this way. Maybe you make money, maybe you don't. But if you don't have a proper framework to apply Almas knowledge you will continue to get random results. A lot of her commentary will only make sense ifyou know and understand a fair amount of markets, instruments, market microstructure. And the nuances. She provides a fair amount of guidance in the longer term and provides also some valuable indicators. But she also tells the technical stuff and the nuances and this is where most people get lost. But this is exactly the stuff I pay for. Because it takes years to learn this and to get to that level. And don’t get me wrong, I have a lot of sympathy for the fact that all of this can be confusing. It also took me years to understand all of this.

comment.body: (Reposting this as for some reason it didn't reply to your comment): That's a fair response and I definitely understand the multitude of variables that are in play when Alma writes her analysis. However, given her analysis, it would seem she put a one dimensional frame on volatility saying spot up/vol up and spot down/vol down under 5565. Now if it was simply explained as "in this environment, I am shorting vol because it is overpriced and is likely to decrease, irregardless of where spot trades here" then that would make a lot more sense as to why she would be shorting vol. But she said very directly, numerous times last Thursday, "spot up-vol up" and "if market rips, that's spot up/volup + low liquidity". So why would you short vol if you were expecting the spot to rally into March OPEX? And why would she also take out call options last Thursday along with shorting vol when those trades go against "spot up-vol up", especially with where spot closed on Thursday (near 5510)? Thursday was a big turning point for this 200 point rally and her short vol trade, and if you were to take her at her analysis at face value, then you'd be behind the 8-ball. Unless you went against it and still won anyways. Let me ask you something... which expression of volatility do you think Alma was referencing last Thursday?

Thursday was a big turning point for this 200 point rally and her short vol trade, and if you were to take her at her analysis at face value, then you'd be behind the 8-ball. Unless you went against it and still won anyways. Let me ask you something... which expression of volatility do you think Alma was referencing last Thursday?

comment.body: That's a fair response and I definitely understand the multitude of variables that are in play when Alma writes her analysis. However, given her analysis, it would seem she put a one dimensional frame on volatility saying spot up/vol up and spot down/vol down under 5565. Now if it was simply explained as "in this environment, I am shorting vol because it is overpriced and is likely to decrease, irregardless of where spot trades here" then that would make a lot more sense as to why she would be shorting vol. But she said very directly, numerous times last Thursday, "spot up-vol up" and "if market rips, that's spot up/volup + low liquidity". So why would you short vol if you were expecting the spot to rally into March OPEX? And why would she also take out call options last Thursday along with shorting vol when those trades go against "spot up-vol up", especially with where spot closed on Thursday (near 5510)? Thursday was a big turning point for this 200 point rally and her short vol trade, and if you were to take her at her analysis at face value, then you'd be behind the 8-ball. Unless you went against it and still won anyways. Let me ask you something... which expression of volatility do you think Alma was referencing last Thursday?
